source_paper_title: How Many Vertices Does a Random Walk Miss in a Network with a Moderately Increasing Number of Vertices?
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Kijima_2026, title={How Many Vertices Does a Random Walk Miss in a Network with a Moderately Increasing Number of Vertices?}, volume={51}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0060}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0060}, number={1}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Kijima, Shuji and Shimizu, Nobutaka and Shiraga, Takeharu}, year={2026}, month=jan, pages={783–805} }

A natural question remains unsettled whether \(E[U] = O(1)\) requires \(d(i) = \Omega(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i))\) for any RWoGG \((d,(G(i))_{i\ge 1},(P^{(i)})_{i\ge 1})\).

A general lower bound of \(E[U]\) is a challenge: a natural question remains unsettled whether \(E[U] = O(1)\) requires \(d(i) = \Omega(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i))\).
title: Characterize when constant expected unvisited vertices requires duration at least hitting time
background_latex:
Consider a growing graph process in discrete time in which, for each integer 
\(i\ge 1\), there is a connected simple undirected graph \(G^{(i)}=(V^{(i)},E^{(i)})\) with \(V^{(i)}=\{v_1,\dots,v_i\}\) and \(G^{(i+1)}\) is obtained from \(G^{(i)}\) by adding one new vertex \(v_{i+1}\) and connecting it to at least one existing vertex (so \(V^{(i)}\subseteq V^{(i+1)}\)).

Let \(d:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}\) be a duration (growth-rate) function: the system stays on \(G^{(i)}\) for exactly \(d(i)\) consecutive random-walk steps before the next vertex is added. Define the change times \(T_1:=0\) and \(T_{i}:=\sum_{j=1}^{i-1} d(j)\) for \(i\ge 2\), so that the vertex set size equals \(i\) during times \(t\in[T_i,T_{i+1})\).

A random walk on this growing graph is specified by transition matrices \(P^{(i)}\in[0,1]^{V^{(i)}\times V^{(i)}}\), where during the period when the current graph is \(G^{(i)}\), the walker evolves as the time-homogeneous Markov chain with transition matrix \(P^{(i)}\). Assume each \(P^{(i)}\) is irreducible (so the walk on \(G^{(i)}\) is well-defined) and define its hitting time
\[
 t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i) \, := \, \max_{u,v\in V^{(i)}} \mathbb{E}\big[\min\{t\ge 0: X_0=u,\ X_t=v\}\big],
\]
where \((X_t)_{t\ge 0}\) denotes a Markov chain with transition matrix \(P^{(i)}\).

Let \((Z_t)_{t\ge 0}\) denote the overall walk on the growing graph (so \(Z_t\in V_t\), and in fact \(Z_t\in V_{t-1}\) for \(t\ge 1\)). Define the number of unvisited vertices just before the \((n+1)\)-st vertex is added by
\[
U(n) \, := \, \left|\, V^{(n)} \setminus \bigcup_{t=0}^{T_{n+1}} \{Z_t\}\,\right|,
\]
and write \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\) for its expectation under the law of the walk determined by \((d,(G^{(i)}),(P^{(i)}))\).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether the following implication holds uniformly over all random walks on growing graphs as defined above:

If \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)] = O(1)\) as \(n\to\infty\), must it be the case that
\[
 d(i) = \Omega\big(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\big) \quad \text{as } i\to\infty?
\]
Equivalently, decide whether there exists a family \((d,(G^{(i)}),(P^{(i)}))\) such that \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\) stays bounded while \(d(i)/t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\to 0\), or prove that no such family exists.
latex:
Consider a growing graph process in discrete time in which, for each integer 
\(i\ge 1\), there is a connected simple undirected graph \(G^{(i)}=(V^{(i)},E^{(i)})\) with \(V^{(i)}=\{v_1,\dots,v_i\}\) and \(G^{(i+1)}\) is obtained from \(G^{(i)}\) by adding one new vertex \(v_{i+1}\) and connecting it to at least one existing vertex (so \(V^{(i)}\subseteq V^{(i+1)}\)).

Let \(d:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}\) be a duration (growth-rate) function: the system stays on \(G^{(i)}\) for exactly \(d(i)\) consecutive random-walk steps before the next vertex is added. Define the change times \(T_1:=0\) and \(T_{i}:=\sum_{j=1}^{i-1} d(j)\) for \(i\ge 2\), so that the vertex set size equals \(i\) during times \(t\in[T_i,T_{i+1})\).

A random walk on this growing graph is specified by transition matrices \(P^{(i)}\in[0,1]^{V^{(i)}\times V^{(i)}}\), where during the period when the current graph is \(G^{(i)}\), the walker evolves as the time-homogeneous Markov chain with transition matrix \(P^{(i)}\). Assume each \(P^{(i)}\) is irreducible (so the walk on \(G^{(i)}\) is well-defined) and define its hitting time
\[
 t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i) \, := \, \max_{u,v\in V^{(i)}} \mathbb{E}\big[\min\{t\ge 0: X_0=u,\ X_t=v\}\big],
\]
where \((X_t)_{t\ge 0}\) denotes a Markov chain with transition matrix \(P^{(i)}\).

Let \((Z_t)_{t\ge 0}\) denote the overall walk on the growing graph (so \(Z_t\in V_t\), and in fact \(Z_t\in V_{t-1}\) for \(t\ge 1\)). Define the number of unvisited vertices just before the \((n+1)\)-st vertex is added by
\[
U(n) \, := \, \left|\, V^{(n)} \setminus \bigcup_{t=0}^{T_{n+1}} \{Z_t\}\,\right|,
\]
and write \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\) for its expectation under the law of the walk determined by \((d,(G^{(i)}),(P^{(i)}))\).

Determine whether the following implication holds uniformly over all random walks on growing graphs as defined above:

If \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)] = O(1)\) as \(n\to\infty\), must it be the case that
\[
 d(i) = \Omega\big(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\big) \quad \text{as } i\to\infty?
\]
Equivalently, decide whether there exists a family \((d,(G^{(i)}),(P^{(i)}))\) such that \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\) stays bounded while \(d(i)/t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\to 0\), or prove that no such family exists.
location_in_paper: Page 5 (end of Section 1.2, discussion after Theorem 1.3) and Page 23, Section 5 Concluding Remarks.
open_problem_quote:
A natural question remains unsettled whether \(E[U] = O(1)\) requires \(d(i) = \Omega(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i))\) for any RWoGG \((d,(G(i))_{i\ge 1},(P^{(i)})_{i\ge 1})\).

A general lower bound of \(E[U]\) is a challenge: a natural question remains unsettled whether \(E[U] = O(1)\) requires \(d(i) = \Omega(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i))\).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: A counterexample would amount to constructing growing graphs and transition rules with provably small \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\) despite durations \(d(i)\ll t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\). Candidate families can be searched computationally on small \(n\) by simulating \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\) and estimating hitting times, but verifying asymptotic separation and ruling out finite-size artifacts is nontrivial.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can encode candidate constructions (graph-growth rules, attachment choices, and possibly transition matrices) and use simulation-based fitness such as small estimated \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\) penalized by large estimated \(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)/d(i)\). This provides an automated feedback signal, but the search space is large and the fitness proxy is noisy and may not correlate with the asymptotic statement.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
In the RWoGG model of Kijima–Shimizu–Shiraga (arXiv:2008.10837), the central sufficient condition for bounded expected unvisited vertices is Theorem 1.2: if there exists \(C>1\) with \(d(i)\ge C\,t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\) for all large \(i\), then \(\mathbb E[U(n)]=O(1)\), and if \(d(i)/t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\to\infty\) then \(\mathbb E[U(n)]\to 0\). The paper also shows that for specific families (complete graphs and paths/Metropolis walks) the scale \(d(i)\asymp t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\) is essentially tight for achieving \(\mathbb E[U(n)]=O(1)\), but it leaves open whether such a lower bound must hold uniformly over all growing-graph processes.

Forward-citing work so far does not resolve this necessity question. The most conceptually adjacent tool is the theory of reversible time-inhomogeneous chains on a fixed vertex set with common stationary distribution, where one can bound the probability of avoiding a vertex over \(T\) steps by \(\exp(-T/t_{\mathrm{HIT}})\) and obtain cover-time bounds in terms of worst static hitting time; however, the fixed-vertex-set and common-\(\pi\) assumptions do not directly apply to vertex growth. Other forward citations develop coupling/monotonicity frameworks (LHaGG/weakly-LHaGG, pausing couplings) and sharp recurrence/transience thresholds for RWoGG on growing trees, hypercubes, and increasing-dimension grids; these results indicate that some global properties (recurrence to a root/origin) can occur with \(d(i)\) far smaller than typical cover times, but they do not control the number of distinct vertices visited.

At present, the implication \(\mathbb E[U(n)]=O(1)\Rightarrow d(i)=\Omega(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i))\) remains open. Promising directions include: (i) attempting to adapt dynamic-graph hitting-time avoidance bounds to the growing-vertex setting by proving that each round begins sufficiently close to stationarity (or by controlling the \(\ell_2(\pi)\) distance as in the original paper’s Theorem 3.6 machinery), and (ii) searching for counterexamples where new vertices attach in a way that makes them easy to hit quickly even though worst-case \(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\) is large (e.g., graphs with a small “gateway” set that the walk visits frequently, while worst-case pairs are separated by bottlenecks).

sources.title: Reversible random walks on dynamic graphs
sources.theorem_or_result: For a time-inhomogeneous random walk on a fixed vertex set with transition matrices \((P_t)_{t\ge1}\) that are irreducible, reversible (often lazy), and share a common stationary distribution \(\pi\), the paper proves uniform bounds on dynamic hitting/cover/coalescing times in terms of the worst static hitting time: in particular, for one walk, \(t_{\mathrm{hit}}((P_t)) = O(\max_t t_{\mathrm{hit}}(P_t))\) (Theorem 1.4(i), with laziness), and \(t_{\mathrm{cov}}((P_t)) = O(\max_t t_{\mathrm{hit}}(P_t)\,\log n)\) (Theorem 1.4(ii)). A key lemma is that if \(X_0\sim\pi\), then for any vertex \(w\) and \(T\ge0\), \(\Pr[\wedge_{t=0}^T\{X_t\neq w\}]\le \prod_{t=1}^T(1-1/t_{\mathrm{hit}}(P_t))\le \exp(-T/t_{\mathrm{HIT}})\) where \(t_{\mathrm{HIT}}:=\max_t t_{\mathrm{hit}}(P_t)\) (Lemma 1.11/4.3).
sources.connection: The open problem concerns a *growing-vertex* graph process with dwell times \(d(i)\) on \(G^{(i)}\), and asks whether bounded expected number of unvisited vertices \(\mathbb E[U(n)]=O(1)\) forces \(d(i)=\Omega(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i))\). This paper studies a different model: dynamic graphs/Markov chains on a *fixed* vertex set, with time-inhomogeneous \(P_t\) but (crucially) a common stationary distribution. It does not analyze vertex growth, the quantity \(U(n)\), nor provide lower bounds relating a dwell time schedule to hitting times. However, its techniques (spectral-radius bounds for killed chains and exponential tail bounds for avoiding a vertex when started from stationarity) could be useful tools when analyzing periods on each \(G^{(i)}\) in the growing-graph setting, especially if one can couple each stage to a chain started near stationarity. Still, it neither proves nor disproves the implication \(\mathbb E[U(n)]=O(1)\Rightarrow d(i)=\Omega(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i))\).

sources.title: An analysis of the recurrence/transience of random walks on growing trees and hypercubes
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves phase transitions for recurrence/transience of random walks on specific growing graphs (complete \(k\)-ary trees, boxes in \(\mathbb Z^d\), and hypercubes \(\{0,1\}^n\)) in the RWoGG model with duration \(d(n)\). For the growing complete \(k\)-ary tree with biased walk parameter \(\lambda\) (simple RW when \(\lambda=1\)), it shows (Theorem 4.2) that the root is recurrent iff \(\sum_{n\ge1} d(n)(\lambda/k)^n=\infty\) (and always recurrent if \(\lambda\ge k\)). For the growing hypercube it shows (Theorem 5.9) that the origin is recurrent iff \(\sum_{n\ge1} d(n)/2^n=\infty\). It introduces the monotonicity/coupling property “less-homesick as graph growing” (LHaGG) and general sufficient conditions for recurrence/transience (Lemmas 3.2–3.5) in terms of stationary mass \(p(n)=\pi_n(v)\) (or even-time stationary mass) and mixing times.
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether bounded expected number of unvisited vertices \(\mathbb E[U(n)]=O(1)\) forces phase durations \(d(i)\) to be at least on the order of the worst-case hitting time \(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\) for each static graph \(G^{(i)}\). This citing paper does not study \(\mathbb E[U(n)]\), cover/unvisited vertices, or relate \(d(i)\) to \(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\). Instead it studies recurrence/transience (return probabilities) on growing graphs and provides coupling-based monotonicity (LHaGG) plus criteria involving stationary probabilities and mixing times. These techniques may be relevant background/tools for analyzing time-inhomogeneous walks on growing graphs, but they do not resolve (or directly address) the implication about unvisited vertices vs hitting time. In particular, their thresholds (e.g., \(d(n)\asymp k^n/(n\log n)\) for recurrence on growing trees) are far smaller than typical hitting/cover times on those graphs, suggesting that recurrence phenomena can occur with \(d(n)\ll t_{\mathrm{hit}}(n)\); however this is about returning to a fixed vertex, not visiting all vertices, so it does not provide a counterexample or proof for the open problem.

sources.title: The Recurrence/Transience of Random Walks on a Bounded Grid in an Increasing Dimension
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves a phase transition for recurrence/transience of a lazy simple random walk on growing boxes \(\{0,\dots,N\}^{n_0+n-1}\) under the RWoGG model: the origin \(o\) is recurrent iff \(\sum_{k\ge1} d(k)/(2N)^k=\infty\), and transient otherwise (Theorem 6). More generally, it gives sufficient conditions for recurrence (Theorem 2: if \(\sum_k d(k)\,\pi^{(k)}(o)=\infty\) under a mild mixing-time summability condition) and for transience (Theorem 4: if \(\sum_{k\ge2} d(k)\,\pi^{(k-1)}(o)<\infty\) assuming a monotonicity property ‘weakly LHaGG’ plus the same mixing condition).
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether bounded expected number of unvisited vertices \(\mathbb E[U(n)]=O(1)\) forces durations \(d(i)\) to be at least on the order of the worst-case hitting time \(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\) uniformly over all growing-graph random walks. This paper does not study \(U(n)\) or cover/miss counts, nor does it relate bounded unvisited vertices to hitting times. Its results concern recurrence/transience (return probabilities to the origin) in the RWoGG framework and introduce coupling/monotonicity tools (weakly LHaGG, pausing coupling) and mixing-time-based criteria. These techniques could be tangentially relevant for analyzing time-inhomogeneous walks, but they do not address whether \(d(i)=\Omega(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i))\) is necessary for \(\mathbb E[U(n)]=O(1)\), nor provide a counterexample with \(d(i)/t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\to0\).

sources.title: 動的グラフ上のランダムウォーク
sources.theorem_or_result: This citing paper is a 2022 Japanese survey article on random walks on dynamic graphs. The only explicit theorem stated about growing-vertex models is Theorem 4.1 (attributed to [17]) for the “increasing coupon collector” (complete graph) model: for duration function \(d\), the expected number of uncollected types at the end of phase \(n\) is \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]=\sum_{i=1}^n\prod_{j=i}^n(1-1/j)^{d(j)}\), and in particular: (i) if \(d(i)/i\ge c>0\) for all \(i\), then \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\le 1/c\) (and if \(d(i)/i\to\infty\) then \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\to 0\)); (ii) if \(d\) is nondecreasing, unbounded, and sublinear then \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]=(1-o(1))\, n/(d(n)+1)\); (iii) if \(d(i)=c\) constant then \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]=(1-O(1/n))\, n/(c+1)\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether bounded \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\) for *arbitrary* growing graphs/Markov chains forces \(d(i)=\Omega(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i))\). This survey does not address that implication. It summarizes known results on dynamic graphs with fixed vertex set (cover/hitting time bounds) and briefly introduces the authors’ SODA’21 work [17] on growing-vertex graphs via an “increasing coupon collector” (complete-graph) special case. In that special case, \(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)=\Theta(i)\) and Theorem 4.1(i) shows bounded \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\) when \(d(i)=\Omega(i)=\Omega(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i))\), which is consistent with (but does not prove) the conjectured necessity. No counterexample with \(d(i)/t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\to 0\) is constructed, and no general necessity theorem is proved.
